text: The surgeon begins dissection at the DJ flexure, proceeding deep to the inferior mesenteric vein. The dissection extends laterally towards the splenic flexure, exposing the left ureter. Medial to lateral dissection at the sacral promontory reveals the inferior mesenteric artery, which is exposed and skeletonized. The pre-sacral space is entered at the level of the sacral promontory.

The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection around the inferior mesenteric artery, which is skeletonized at its origin from the abdominal aorta. The inferior mesenteric artery is double clipped and divided, followed by the double clipping and division of the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, exposing the left ureter. Concurrently, a lateral to medial dissection begins at the pelvic brim and extends towards the splenic flexure, with the intention of joining these dissection spaces.

The surgeon continues dissection on both the left and right sides of the rectum towards the pelvis. Posterior and pre-sacral dissection progresses in the TMA plane, while anterior dissection involves dividing the peritoneal reflection in the Douglas pouch. The TME dissection advances towards the pelvic floor. The rectum is stapled and divided below the tumor using an endo-GIA tri-stapler, potentially requiring multiple firings. The mesentery of the descending colon is divided at the level of the proximal resection.

The surgical specimen is externalized, completing the resection. A purse-string suture is applied, and an EEA circular stapler is introduced and opened. The anvil is securely clicked into the stapler, which is then closed and fired. The anastomosis is tested underwater for leaks. A tube drain is inserted, and a loop of terminal ileum is selected for ileostomy, followed by washout.
